<!-- GENERATED by scripts/sync_agent_assets.py from agents/skills/foam/references/mesh-quality.md — edit the source and re-run the script; do not edit this copy. -->

# Mesh quality judgement (on top of assess_mesh)

`assess_mesh(case_dir)` runs `checkMesh -allTopology -allGeometry` on the
case and returns typed mesh-quality facts — numbers computed from the
output, never guessed, at the same cost for a 400-cell toy and a
million-cell mesh. It reads the mesh as checkMesh sees it, so it works
unchanged on blockMesh, GMSH-imported and snappyHexMesh meshes.
A case with no mesh, or a checkMesh crash, raises a typed error — never a
fabricated assessment.

You get: `census` (points, faces, internal_faces, cells, boundary_patches
and the `cell_types` breakdown — hexahedra, prisms, tetrahedra, polyhedra,
...), `metrics` — one entry per parsed check with a stable snake_case
`name`, its `value` where checkMesh reports one, `check` (`"topology"` or
`"geometry"`), `checkmesh_ok` (checkMesh's own mark) and a `classification`
in {pass, warn, fail} — plus `failed_checks` (checkMesh's own reported
count), `mesh_ok` (True iff checkMesh concluded "Mesh OK."), `flags` (the
checkMesh flags actually used, read from the run's own Exec line), and a
`verdict` in {ok, warnings, failed} with `evidence` strings naming what
drove it.

**The classification applies conservative mechanical defaults; the
judgement layer is this document.** The built-in rules:

- checkMesh's failure marks are ground truth: every `***`-failed check is
  `fail`, and the verdict is never better than checkMesh's own conclusion
  ("Mesh OK." vs "Failed N mesh checks.").
- A warn band flags marginal-but-legal values on top:
  `max_non_orthogonality` from 65 degrees (checkMesh v10 marks faces above
  70 as severe and fails the check only above 90), `max_skewness` from 2
  (v10 fails above 4), `max_aspect_ratio` from 100 (v10 fails above 1000).
  checkMesh's own single-`*` notices (multiple regions, severely
  non-orthogonal faces) also read as `warn`.
- Zero parsed checks never classify `ok`: the verdict is `failed` with
  evidence saying why (the vacuous-pass trap).

## What each metric means — and what a bad value does to a solution

- **`max_non_orthogonality`** (degrees): the angle between each internal
  face's normal and the line joining the two cell centres it separates.
  The FV Laplacian assumes the two are aligned; the misalignment is patched
  by explicit non-orthogonal corrections, which are themselves approximate.
  High values degrade gradients and diffusion terms, demand
  `nNonOrthogonalCorrectors` (1–2) and limited schemes
  (`laplacian ... Gauss linear limited corrected 0.33;`), slow convergence,
  and past ~70–80 are a leading divergence cause.
- **`max_skewness`**: how far the line between two adjacent cell centres
  misses the centre of the face they share (normalized). Face values are
  interpolated at the wrong point, smearing gradients (a false-diffusion
  error) and destabilizing higher-order schemes — skewed faces are where
  transient runs blow up first.
- **`max_aspect_ratio`**: longest-to-shortest cell dimension. Harmless when
  the flow is aligned with the long direction (wall-aligned boundary-layer
  cells); poison for isotropic physics — it stiffens the pressure equation
  and amplifies cross-stream diffusion errors.
- **`min_face_area` / `min_volume`**: degenerate or inverted cells. A
  negative volume means the mesh is inside-out somewhere — a construction
  error, not a quality issue; regenerate. Near-zero cells also cap the
  usable `deltaT` (Courant scales with cell size).
- **`max_cell_openness`**: whether each cell's faces actually close it
  (healthy values are machine-precision, ~1e-16). A finite value means
  broken face construction — nothing downstream is trustworthy.
- **`min_edge_length`**: sliver edges beget sliver faces and cells —
  roundoff-scale geometry that poisons gradients and time steps.
- **Topology checks** (`check: "topology"` — points/faces/cells
  consistency, `point_usage`, `number_of_regions`, boundary definition,
  cell-to-face connectivity, ...): these say "the mesh is MALFORMED", not
  "the mesh is low quality". A topology failure means regenerate or fix the
  conversion — sizing knobs will not help. `number_of_regions` > 1 (a
  `warn` notice) means the mesh splits into disconnected pieces — a
  generation bug until proven intentional (a deliberate multi-region case).

## Per-application thresholds (the warn band is generic; your run is not)

The warn band (65 / 2 / 100) is a deliberately conservative mechanical
default — it exists to make you look, not to decide for you. Judge a
`warnings` verdict against what the run is for:

| Application | non-orthogonality | skewness | aspect ratio | posture on `warnings` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Steady RANS estimate (trends, ballpark) | up to ~70 workable with 1–2 non-orthogonal correctors and limited schemes | < 4 tolerable | O(100)+ fine inside wall-aligned layers | usually proceed; state the caveat in the report |
| Steady RANS, accuracy matters (forces to trust, grid study) | < 60 | < 2 | < 100, near-isotropic outside layers | treat each warning as a to-fix before trusting numbers |
| Transient (PISO/PIMPLE, tight deltaT) | < 60 — corrections cost every step and destabilize | < 2 | moderate — stretched/tiny cells cap deltaT via Courant | fix warnings unless the marginal cells are far from the action |
| LES / DES | < 50 | < ~1 | near-isotropic (< 10) away from walls | the warn band is far too lax here — remesh, don't rationalize |

VOF (interFoam family): wherever the interface travels, hold the transient
row's skewness and non-orthogonality — interface compression amplifies mesh
error into wisps and parasitic currents.

## Which knob to turn

Geometry failures and warn-band values map to mesh-generation parameters.
For snappy-built meshes, [references/snappyhexmesh.md](snappyhexmesh.md)
carries the full downgrade ladder and the concave-cells reading rule; the
column here is the knob summary.

| Failing metric | blockMesh | GMSH | snappyHexMesh |
|---|---|---|---|
| `max_non_orthogonality` | milder `simpleGrading` ratios (expansion <= ~3 per block); move block vertices so edges meet near 90 degrees; split strongly sheared blocks | cleaner geometry; structured/transfinite meshing where possible; refine near curvature (Distance + Threshold size fields); try another 2D/3D `Mesh.Algorithm` | more castellated refinement before snap; gentler snapControls (`nSmoothPatch`, `nRelaxIter`, `tolerance`) |
| `max_skewness` | reposition vertices to unshear blocks; milder grading; more cells across curved blocks | smaller characteristic length in the flagged region (the same Distance + Threshold fields used for local refinement); enable mesh optimization | snapControls smoothing; raise the castellation level at the offending surface |
| `max_aspect_ratio` | rebalance per-direction cell counts; reduce grading expansion | shrink the wall-size vs far-field-size gap in the Threshold field | addLayersControls: `expansionRatio`, `finalLayerThickness`, `minThickness` |
| `min_face_area` / `min_volume` | check vertex numbering (right-handed blocks) and collapsed edges in `blockMeshDict` | fix surface orientation, remove sliver surfaces, verify the extrusion | clean the geometry (self-intersections, gaps); meshQualityControls |
| `min_edge_length` | collapsed or near-duplicate vertices | merge near-duplicate geometry points; set a minimum mesh size | feature-edge cleanup; `resolveFeatureAngle` |
| `max_cell_openness` | should not happen from blockMesh — report it | broken face construction: reconvert (`gmshToFoam`), verify `Mesh.MshFileVersion` 2.2 | regenerate |
| `point_usage` (unused points) | should not happen from blockMesh — report it | conversion artifact: regenerate the `.msh` and reconvert; ensure exactly one physical volume group | regenerate |
| `number_of_regions` > 1 | blocks not sharing vertices exactly — merge duplicate vertex coordinates | a stray disconnected volume, or a missing/extra physical volume group | castellation leaked through a geometry gap — close it |
| any other `check: "topology"` failure | regenerate — the mesh is malformed, not low-quality | fix the script or reconvert; never tune sizing for a topology failure | regenerate |

## Reading the verdict and evidence

- `ok` — evidence is "checkMesh concluded: Mesh OK." plus "all N parsed
  checks pass". Proceed — but judge the census too: `cells` against the
  resolution the physics needs (a legal 400-cell mesh answers no
  boundary-layer question), `cell_types` for what to expect (tet-heavy GMSH
  meshes run higher skewness and non-orthogonality than hex-dominant ones).
- `warnings` — one evidence string per marginal metric, in one of two
  shapes: "`<name> = <value>` is at/above the warn threshold `<band>`
  (legal but marginal)" → judge with the table above; "`<name>` flagged by
  checkMesh with a '*' notice" → checkMesh's own attention mark (multiple
  regions, severely non-orthogonal faces) — disconnected regions are a bug
  until proven intentional.
- `failed` — evidence opens with "checkMesh itself concluded: Failed N mesh
  checks." and names each failed metric — with its value where checkMesh
  reported one — as "(topology check)" or "(geometry check)". Geometry failures → the knob table.
  Topology failures → regenerate or fix the conversion. Never start a
  solver on a `failed` mesh — that run is the divergence the convergence
  reference will later report.
- Evidence "checkMesh reported N failed checks but only M failure lines
  were parsed — read the raw log" → the parser missed a failure line; fall
  back to the raw output: `run_openfoam_command(case_dir, "checkMesh")`.
- Evidence "no mesh quality checks could be parsed ..." → same fallback.
- A typed error instead of an assessment → no mesh yet (generate it first)
  or checkMesh itself crashed; the raw-`checkMesh` fallback applies.

`flags` records the exact checkMesh flags the assessment ran with
(`-allTopology -allGeometry` today), so a stricter or looser future variant
is distinguishable in conversation.
